The 'casting out' (ekblethesetai) uses the exorcism verb -- the cross is the cosmic exorcism, the judgment of Psalm 82:8 ('Arise, O God, judge the earth!') executed through the death and resurrection of the Son. Third, 12:41: 'Isaiah said these things because he saw his glory and spoke of him.' John identifies the YHWH whom Isaiah saw 'high and lifted up' in the Temple vision (Isa 6:1-5) as Jesus. This means Isaiah's vision of the divine council throne room -- with the seraphim crying 'Holy, holy, holy is YHWH of hosts' -- was a vision of the pre-incarnate Logos. The Logos is the YHWH of Isaiah 6, the visible God whom the prophets encountered when they were granted access to the divine council.

sections.heading: The Raising of Lazarus -- The Seventh Sign (11:1-57)
sections.body: The structure of chapter 11 is masterful. Lazarus of Bethany is sick; his sisters Martha and Mary send word to Jesus: 'Lord, he whom you love is ill' (11:3). Jesus deliberately delays two days (11:6), then announces: 'Lazarus has died, and for your sake I am glad that I was not there, so that you may believe' (11:14-15). By the time Jesus arrives, Lazarus has been in the tomb four days. Martha meets Jesus and professes faith in the final resurrection (11:24). Jesus' response transcends her expectation: 'I am the resurrection and the life' (11:25). He does not say 'I will perform the resurrection' or 'I will give you resurrection.' He says he IS resurrection -- the power over death is not a capability he possesses but his very nature. Martha's confession (11:27) is the fullest christological statement in the Gospels before the crucifixion. When Mary comes weeping, 'Jesus wept' (edakrysen, 11:35). The incarnate God who IS the resurrection and the life weeps at the tomb of his friend. His grief is not ignorance of the outcome but a genuine sharing of human anguish at death -- the enemy that has no right to exist in God's good creation. At the tomb, he commands the stone removed. Martha warns: 'Lord, by this time there will be an odor, for he has been dead four days' (11:39). Jesus prays to the Father (for the crowd's sake, 11:42) and cries out 'with a loud voice (phone megale), "Lazarus, come out!"' (11:43). The specificity of the name is noted by the church fathers: had Jesus simply said 'Come out!' all the dead might have risen. Lazarus emerges, still wrapped in burial cloths. 'Unbind him, and let him go' (11:44). The ironic result: the supreme sign of life triggers the decision to pursue Jesus' death. The Sanhedrin convenes, and Caiaphas speaks his unwitting prophecy (11:49-52).

sections.heading: The Arrival of the Hour -- Anointing, Entry, and Judgment (12:1-50)
sections.body: Six days before Passover, Mary anoints Jesus' feet with 'a pound of expensive ointment made from pure nard' (12:3) -- worth a year's wages. Jesus defends her: 'She has kept it for the day of my burial' (12:7). The anointing anticipates his death; the house 'was filled with the fragrance' (12:3) -- a sensory image of the gospel's spread. The triumphal entry (12:12-19) fulfills Zechariah 9:9: the king comes on a donkey, not a war horse -- a humble king, not a military conqueror. Greeks come seeking Jesus (12:20-22), prompting his declaration: 'The hour has come for the Son of Man to be glorified' (12:23). The 'hour' (hora) that was 'not yet' at Cana (2:4), that the authorities could not precipitate (7:30; 8:20), has now arrived. Jesus states the cosmic principle: 'Unless a grain of wheat falls into the earth and dies, it remains alone; but if it dies, it bears much fruit' (12:24). Then the soul's anguish: 'Now is my soul troubled. And what shall I say? "Father, save me from this hour"? But for this purpose I have come to this hour. Father, glorify your name' (12:27-28). A voice from heaven responds: 'I have glorified it, and I will glorify it again' (12:28). Then the climactic divine council declaration: 'Now is the judgment of this world; now will the ruler of this world be cast out. And I, when I am lifted up from the earth, will draw all people to myself' (12:31-32). The cross is the divine council's judgment on the hostile archon. The lifting up of the Son of Man is the mechanism by which the ruler's authority is broken and 'all people' -- not just Israel -- are drawn to the true King. The Book of Signs closes with John's theological retrospective: 'Isaiah said these things because he saw his glory (doxa) and spoke of him' (12:41) -- identifying the YHWH of Isaiah 6 as the pre-incarnate Christ. Despite the signs, many did not believe (12:37); yet 'many even of the authorities believed in him, but for fear of the Pharisees they did not confess it, so that they would not be put out of the synagogue (aposynagogoi)' (12:42). The Book of Signs ends with light and darkness in tension, the hour arrived, the ruler of this world about to be judged.

john_glory.title: The Foot-Washing and Judas's Departure -- The Hour Has Come
john_glory.synopsis: Chapter 13 opens the Book of Glory with one of the most dramatic scenes in the Gospels. 'Now before the Feast of the Passover, when Jesus knew that his hour (hora) had come to depart out of this world to the Father, having loved his own (tous idious) who were in the world, he loved them to the end (eis telos)' (13:1). The phrase eis telos means both 'to the uttermost' and 'to the end/completion' -- his love is both supreme in degree and final in purpose. John sets the scene with theological precision: 'Jesus, knowing that the Father had given all things into his hands, and that he had come from God and was going back to God (apo theou exelthen kai pros ton theon hypagei)' (13:3). The one who came from the divine council's realm and is returning to it -- the Logos who was 'with God' (1:1) -- now takes off his outer garment, wraps a towel around his waist, and washes his disciples' feet (13:4-5). Peter protests: 'You shall never wash my feet!' Jesus responds: 'If I do not wash you, you have no share with me (ouk echeis meros met emou)' (13:8). The washing symbolizes both the cleansing of the cross and the model of servant leadership: 'If I then, your Lord and Teacher, have washed your feet, you also ought to wash one another's feet' (13:14). The scene then turns dark. Jesus identifies his betrayer: 'One of you will betray me' (13:21). The Beloved Disciple, reclining 'at Jesus' side' (en to kolpo tou Iesou -- 13:23, echoing 1:18 where the Son is 'in the bosom of the Father'), asks who it is. Jesus dips a morsel and gives it to Judas Iscariot. 'After he received the morsel, Satan entered into him (eiselthen eis ekeinon ho Satanas)' (13:27). Jesus says: 'What you are going to do, do quickly' (13:27). Judas goes out -- 'and it was night (nyx)' (13:30). The darkness is both literal and symbolic: the agent of the ruler of this world departs into the realm of darkness to set in motion the hour of glory. Jesus gives the 'new commandment' (entole kaine): 'Love one another as I have loved you' (13:34). He predicts Peter's denial (13:36-38). A final ego eimi appears: 'I tell you this now, before it takes place, that when it does take place you may believe that I AM (ego eimi)' (13:19).

key_verse.text: Jesus, knowing that the Father had given all things into his hands, and that he had come from God and was going back to God, rose from supper. He laid aside his outer garments, and taking a towel, tied it around his waist. Then he poured water into a basin and began to wash the disciples' feet.

original_terms: hora (hour -- the divinely appointed moment of Jesus' death and glorification; now arrived; the culmination of the Gospel's temporal framework: 2:4; 7:30; 8:20; 12:23; 13:1; 17:1)
eis telos (to the end/to the uttermost -- 13:1; his love reaches its final, complete expression; the cross is the eis telos of divine love)
meros (share/portion -- 13:8; 'you have no share with me'; echoes the OT inheritance language; to be washed by Jesus is to receive a share in his life)
en to kolpo (in the bosom/at the side -- 13:23; the Beloved Disciple's position with Jesus mirrors the Son's position with the Father, 1:18; intimate proximity)
ho Satanas (Satan -- 13:27; 'Satan entered into him'; the hostile spiritual power who motivates Judas's betrayal; the 'ruler of this world' acting through a human agent)
entole kaine (new commandment -- 13:34; 'love one another as I have loved you'; new not in content but in standard: the measure is Jesus' sacrificial love)
ego eimi (I AM -- 13:19; the final absolute ego eimi in the discourse section; Jesus foretells events 'so that you may believe that I AM')
john_glory.ane_backdrop: Foot-washing in the ancient Near East was a task reserved for the lowest servant or slave. In the ANE household, guests' feet were washed upon arrival -- but by servants, not by the host. The Talmud (b. Qiddushin 22b) specifies that foot-washing was so demeaning that a Hebrew slave could not be required to do it -- only a Gentile slave or a wife (voluntarily) would perform this task. By washing the disciples' feet, Jesus enacts the theology of Philippians 2:5-8: the one who had 'all things in his hands' (13:3), who came from God and was going to God, takes the form of the lowest servant. The divine council's Lord becomes the household's slave. The dipping of the morsel for Judas may reflect the ANE custom of the host honoring a guest by selecting food for them -- making the betrayal all the more treacherous.

second_temple.summary: 'Christ Jesus, who, though he was in the form of God, did not count equality with God a thing to be grasped, but emptied himself, by taking the form of a servant, being born in the likeness of men. And being found in human form, he humbled himself by becoming obedient to the point of death.'
second_temple.relevance: Paul's kenosis hymn is the theological parallel to John 13's foot-washing. Both express the same pattern: the one who existed in divine glory (John: 'came from God'; Phil: 'in the form of God') chose to serve at the lowest level (John: washing feet; Phil: 'form of a servant').

second_temple.summary: 'Who is the greater, one who reclines at table or one who serves? Is it not the one who reclines at table? But I am among you as the one who serves.'
second_temple.relevance: The Synoptic tradition preserves Jesus' teaching about servant leadership. John dramatizes it in the foot-washing -- the acted parable of 13:3-17 IS Luke 22:27 embodied.

second_temple.summary: Satan as a figure who incites betrayal (1 Chr 21:1, David's census) and as a member of the divine council who challenges God's governance (Job 1-2).
second_temple.relevance: 'Satan entered into him' (13:27) shows the hostile council member operating through a human agent. Judas's betrayal is not merely human treachery but a move in the cosmic conflict between the divine council's Lord and the ruler of this world.

cross_refs.note: The kenosis hymn: equality with God, self-emptying, servant's form -- the theological parallel to the foot-washing
'Then Satan entered into Judas called Iscariot' -- the Synoptic parallel to 13:27
'Even my close friend in whom I trusted, who ate my bread, has lifted his heel against me' -- quoted by Jesus in 13:18
'Love your neighbor as yourself' -- the old commandment that Jesus' 'new commandment' (13:34) transcends: love not as you love yourself but as HE loved you
'By this we know love, that he laid down his life for us, and we ought to lay down our lives for the brothers' -- the Johannine community applying the new commandment
The Synoptic account of the betrayal prediction at the Last Supper

john_glory.divine_council_note: John 13 dramatizes the divine council paradox: the one who holds 'all things in his hands' (13:3) -- the Logos through whom all things were made (1:3), the Son to whom the Father has given 'all judgment' (5:22) and 'all authority' (cf. Matt 28:18) -- becomes the foot-washing slave. This is the divine council's Lord inverting every expectation of power. The Son who came 'from God' and is going 'back to God' (13:3) -- the round trip from the heavenly council to the world and back -- pauses at the lowest point of human service before ascending to the highest point of cosmic glory. The entry of Satan into Judas (13:27) reveals the spiritual dimension of the betrayal: this is not merely human politics but the hostile council member making his move. The 'ruler of this world' (14:30) acts through Judas to bring about the crucifixion -- but in doing so, he unwittingly triggers his own judgment (12:31; 16:11). The ego eimi of 13:19 has a prophetic function: Jesus foretells the betrayal so that 'when it takes place, you may believe that I AM.' Foreknowledge of events proves divine identity -- only the God who presides over the council and governs history can announce events before they happen (Isa 46:10).

sections.heading: The Foot-Washing (13:1-17)
sections.body: The chapter opens with the most comprehensive christological summary in the Gospel: Jesus knows (1) his hour has come, (2) he is departing to the Father, (3) the Father has given all things to him, (4) he came from God, (5) he is going to God. Knowing all this, he washes feet. The juxtaposition is the theology: supreme knowledge and supreme authority expressed in supreme humility. Peter's protest (13:6-8) and Jesus' response reveal that the foot-washing is not merely an example of service but a symbol of the cleansing that the cross will accomplish. 'The one who has bathed does not need to wash, except for his feet, but is completely clean' (13:10). Those who have been cleansed by Jesus' sacrificial death need ongoing cleansing from the daily dirt of living in a fallen world. Jesus then draws the ethical application: 'I have given you an example (hypodeigma), that you also should do just as I have done to you. Truly, truly, I say to you, a servant is not greater than his master' (13:15-16). The community of disciples is to be marked by the servant love that the divine council's Lord has modeled.

sections.heading: The Betrayal and the New Commandment (13:18-38)
sections.body: Jesus quotes Psalm 41:9: 'He who ate my bread has lifted his heel against me' (13:18). The betrayal fulfills scripture. 'I am telling you this now, before it takes place, so that when it does take place you may believe that ego eimi' (13:19). Foreknowledge proves deity. 'Truly, truly, I say to you, whoever receives the one I send receives me, and whoever receives me receives the one who sent me' (13:20) -- the chain of sending from the divine council: the Father sends the Son, the Son sends the disciples, and receiving the messenger is receiving the sender. Jesus is 'troubled in his spirit' (13:21) and reveals the betrayer through the morsel. 'Satan entered into him' (13:27) -- the moment of cosmic invasion. Judas departs: 'and it was night' (13:30). The departure of the betrayer is simultaneously the departure of darkness from the circle of light. With Judas gone, Jesus announces: 'Now is the Son of Man glorified, and God is glorified in him' (13:31). The glorification begins at the moment the betrayal is set in motion -- the cross is glory, not defeat. The 'new commandment' (entole kaine, 13:34) is new not because love is new but because the standard is new: 'as I have loved you' -- sacrificial, foot-washing, cross-bearing love. 'By this all people will know that you are my disciples, if you have love for one another' (13:35). Peter declares he will lay down his life; Jesus predicts three denials before the rooster crows (13:37-38).

john_glory.title: The Farewell Discourse -- The Way, the Parakletos, and the Vine
john_glory.synopsis: Chapters 14-16 comprise the Farewell Discourse (sometimes called the 'Upper Room Discourse') -- Jesus' final extended teaching before his death, addressed to the eleven remaining disciples. The discourse is structured around three major themes: (1) the departure and return of Jesus, (2) the coming of the Parakletos (Advocate/ Helper/Comforter), and (3) the relationship of the disciples to Jesus, the Father, and the hostile world. Chapter 14 opens with comfort: 'Let not your hearts be troubled. Believe in God; believe also in me. In my Father's house are many rooms (monai). If it were not so, would I have told you that I go to prepare a place for you?' (14:1-2). The 'Father's house' with 'many rooms' evokes the divine council's heavenly dwelling -- the cosmic temple where the Father resides and where the Son is going. Thomas asks how to find the way; Jesus responds with the sixth I AM statement: 'I am the way (he hodos), and the truth (he aletheia), and the life (he zoe). No one comes to the Father except through me' (14:6). Philip asks to see the Father; Jesus replies: 'Whoever has seen me has seen the Father' (14:9). The Parakletos is introduced: 'I will ask the Father, and he will give you another Advocate (allon parakleton), to be with you forever, even the Spirit of truth' (14:16-17). The 'another' (allon -- another of the same kind) implies Jesus himself was the first Parakletos. Chapter 15 presents the seventh I AM statement: 'I am the true vine (he ampelos he alethine), and my Father is the vinedresser' (15:1). Branches that bear fruit are pruned; those that do not are removed. 'Abide in me, and I in you' (15:4). The love commandment deepens: 'Greater love has no one than this, that someone lay down his life for his friends' (15:13). The world's hatred is explained: 'If the world hates you, know that it has hated me before it hated you... If they persecuted me, they will also persecute you' (15:18-20). Jesus quotes Psalm 69:4: 'They hated me without a cause' (15:25). Chapter 16 warns of persecution: 'They will put you out of the synagogues (aposynagogous). Indeed, the hour is coming when whoever kills you will think he is offering service to God' (16:2). The Parakletos's ministry is described: 'When he comes, he will convict the world concerning sin and righteousness and judgment: concerning sin, because they do not believe in me; concerning righteousness, because I go to the Father; concerning judgment, because the ruler of this world is judged' (16:8-11). Jesus promises: 'A little while, and you will see me no longer; and again a little while, and you will see me' (16:16). The discourse concludes: 'In the world you will have tribulation. But take heart; I have overcome the world (ego nenikeka ton kosmon)' (16:33).

key_verse.text: Jesus said to him, 'I am the way, and the truth, and the life. No one comes to the Father except through me.'... '...concerning judgment, because the ruler of this world is judged.'

original_terms: he hodos kai he aletheia kai he zoe (the way, the truth, and the life -- the sixth I AM + predicate; Jesus is the exclusive access to the Father; not a teacher of the way but THE way)

parakletos (Advocate/Helper/Comforter -- from parakaleo, 'to call alongside'; a legal term for a defense attorney; the Spirit is 'another Parakletos' of the same kind as Jesus; 14:16, 26; 15:26; 16:7)
to pneuma tes aletheias (the Spirit of truth -- 14:17; 15:26; 16:13; the Spirit's ministry is to guide into 'all the truth' and to glorify Jesus)

he ampelos he alethine (the true vine -- the seventh I AM + predicate; Israel was God's vine (Ps 80:8-16; Isa 5:1-7; Jer 2:21) that failed; Jesus is the TRUE vine, the faithful Israel; connection to him defines God's people)
menein (to abide/remain/dwell -- 15:4-10; used 11 times in the vine passage alone; the foundational Johannine concept of continuous relational union with Christ)
ho archon tou kosmou toutou (the ruler of this world -- 14:30; 16:11; the hostile spiritual power who is 'coming' (14:30) and is 'judged' (kekritai, perfect tense, 16:11))
nenikeka ton kosmon (I have overcome/conquered the world -- 16:33; perfect tense: the victory is accomplished and its effects endure)
john_glory.ane_backdrop: The farewell discourse as a literary genre has deep roots in the ancient Near East and in Jewish tradition. The 'testamentary discourse' -- a dying leader's final instructions -- appears in Jacob's blessings (Gen 49), Moses' farewell (Deut 31-34), Joshua's farewell (Josh 23-24), David's final words (1 Kings 2; 1 Chr 28-29), and in Second Temple literature (the Testaments of the Twelve Patriarchs, the Testament of Moses). These farewells typically include: (1) prediction of the leader's departure, (2) review of the past, (3) commands for the future, (4) prediction of future troubles, (5) designation of a successor, and (6) a prayer/blessing. John's Farewell Discourse follows this pattern but transforms it: the successor is not a human leader but the Parakletos -- the Spirit of truth who will continue Jesus' presence. The vine imagery (chapter 15) connects to the widespread ANE and Israelite symbolism of the vine as the people of God, the vineyard as the land, and the vinedresser as God.

second_temple.summary: The Rule of the Community describes the 'Spirit of Truth' (ruach emet) who leads the 'sons of light' and will ultimately purify them, and the 'Angel of Truth' (malakh emet) who aids them.
second_temple.relevance: John's 'Spirit of truth' (to pneuma tes aletheias -- 14:17; 15:26; 16:13) uses the same terminology as the Qumran community. Both share the dualistic framework of truth vs. falsehood, light vs. darkness, but John personalizes the Spirit of truth as the Parakletos sent by the Father and the Son.

second_temple.summary: Each patriarch gathers his sons, reviews his life, predicts the future, gives ethical instructions, and blesses or warns. Common themes include predictions of apostasy, exile, and restoration.
second_temple.relevance: The testamentary genre provides the literary framework for John 14-16. Jesus is the greater patriarch whose farewell surpasses all predecessors: he sends not a human successor but the divine Parakletos.

second_temple.summary: 'My beloved had a vineyard on a very fertile hill... and he looked for it to yield grapes, but it yielded wild grapes... the vineyard of the LORD of hosts is the house of Israel.'
second_temple.relevance: Isaiah's vineyard song describes Israel as God's vine that produced bad fruit. Jesus claims: 'I am the TRUE vine' (15:1) -- the faithful Israel, the vine that produces the fruit God intended. Connection to Jesus, not ethnic Israel, defines the covenant people.

cross_refs.note: 'You brought a vine out of Egypt; you drove out the nations and planted it' -- Israel as God's vine; Jesus as the 'true vine' fulfills and replaces it
The vineyard song: Israel as the vine that failed; Jesus as the true vine (15:1) that bears the fruit God desired
'Those who hate me without a cause' -- quoted by Jesus in 15:25 to explain the world's irrational hatred

'You cannot see my face, for man shall not see me and live' -- Philip's request 'show us the Father' (14:8) and Jesus' reply 'whoever has seen me has seen the Father' (14:9) resolves this: the visible God is the Son
Moses' farewell discourse: 'I am no longer able to go out and come in... the LORD your God himself will go over before you' -- the prototype for Jesus' Farewell Discourse
'You will receive power when the Holy Spirit has come upon you, and you will be my witnesses' -- the fulfillment of the Parakletos promise
'The Spirit himself intercedes for us' -- Paul's understanding of the Parakletos's advocacy
'If anyone does sin, we have an advocate (parakleton) with the Father, Jesus Christ the righteous' -- Jesus as the first Parakletos, the Spirit as 'another'
'We do not wrestle against flesh and blood, but against the rulers, against the authorities' -- the spiritual warfare context for the 'ruler of this world' being judged (16:11)

john_glory.divine_council_note: The Farewell Discourse is saturated with divine council theology. First, 14:2: 'In my Father's house (oikia) are many rooms (monai).' The 'Father's house' is the divine council's heavenly dwelling -- the cosmic temple where YHWH presides (Ps 11:4; Isa 6:1). The 'many rooms' may echo the heavenly temple's architecture and the multiple thrones of Daniel 7:9 -- there is space in the divine council's domain for those who belong to Jesus. Second, 14:6: 'No one comes to the Father except through me.' The Son is the exclusive access point to the Father's throne room. In the OT, the divine council was accessed only by prophets granted visions (1 Kings 22; Isa 6; Ezek 1; Dan 7). Now the access is permanent and personal -- through the Son. Third, 14:9: 'Whoever has seen me has seen the Father.' This resolves the 'two powers' tension: the visible God (the Son, the Logos) perfectly reveals the invisible God (the Father). No one has seen the Father directly (1:18); every theophany, every vision of God, was an encounter with the Son. Fourth, the Parakletos (14:16-17, 26; 15:26; 16:7-15): the Spirit of truth is sent by the Father at the Son's request (14:16) or by the Son from the Father (15:26). The Parakletos proceeds 'from the Father' (para tou patros ekporeuetai -- 15:26), a phrase that became central to the Nicene Creed's pneumatology. The Spirit's ministry is to (a) teach and remind (14:26), (b) bear witness to Jesus (15:26), (c) convict the world of sin, righteousness, and judgment (16:8-11), and (d) guide into all truth and glorify Jesus (16:13-14). The Spirit does not speak 'on his own authority' but speaks 'whatever he hears' (16:13) -- echoing the divine council pattern where messengers relay the council's decrees. Fifth, 16:11: 'the ruler of this world is judged (kekritai).' The perfect tense indicates a completed verdict with ongoing effect. The hostile archon has been tried and sentenced in the divine council; the cross is the execution of that sentence. The disciples' future persecution (16:2) is the dying thrash of a judged enemy.

sections.heading: The Way, the Truth, and the Life (14:1-31)
sections.body: 'Let not your hearts be troubled' (14:1) -- comfort in the face of departure. Jesus goes to 'prepare a place' in the Father's house (14:2-3) and will return to take the disciples to himself. Thomas asks: 'Lord, we do not know where you are going. How can we know the way?' (14:5). Jesus' answer is the sixth I AM statement: 'I am the way, and the truth, and the life. No one comes to the Father except through me' (14:6). Three claims in one: (a) Jesus is THE way -- not a way among many but the exclusive path to the Father; (b) Jesus is THE truth -- not merely a teacher of true things but truth personified, the revelation of ultimate reality; (c) Jesus is THE life -- the source from which all life flows (cf. 1:4, 'In him was life'). Philip asks: 'Lord, show us the Father, and it is enough for us' (14:8). Jesus' reply carries immense divine council significance: 'Have I been with you so long, and you still do not know me, Philip? Whoever has seen me has seen the Father' (14:9). The visible God (the Son) IS the revelation of the invisible God (the Father). The mutual indwelling is stated: 'I am in the Father and the Father is in me' (14:10-11). The Parakletos is promised: 'another Advocate' (14:16) -- the Spirit of truth who 'dwells with you and will be in you' (14:17). Jesus promises: 'I will not leave you as orphans (orphanous); I will come to you' (14:18). 'The one who loves me will be loved by my Father, and I will love him and manifest (emphaniso) myself to him' (14:21). 'If anyone loves me, he will keep my word, and my Father will love him, and we will come to him and make our home (monen) with him' (14:23) -- the Father and Son take up residence in the believer, fulfilling the tabernacling presence of 1:14. 'Peace (eirene) I leave with you; my peace I give to you' (14:27). The chapter ends: 'the ruler of this world is coming. He has no claim on me (en emoi ouk echei ouden)' (14:30).

sections.heading: The True Vine and the World's Hatred (15:1-27)
sections.body: 'I am the true vine (he ampelos he alethine), and my Father is the vinedresser (ho georgos)' (15:1). The seventh and final I AM + predicate statement. Israel was God's vine (Ps 80; Isa 5; Jer 2:21; Ezek 15; Hos 10:1) -- planted, tended, and expected to bear fruit. But Israel produced 'wild grapes' (Isa 5:4). Jesus is the 'true' vine -- the faithful Israel, the vine that bears the fruit God intended. The key verb is menein ('abide/remain/dwell'), used 11 times in 15:4-10. 'Abide in me, and I in you. As the branch cannot bear fruit by itself, unless it abides in the vine, neither can you, unless you abide in me' (15:4). Fruitfulness depends entirely on connection to Christ. Branches that bear fruit are pruned (kathairei -- the same root as 'clean,' katharos); branches that do not are removed and burned (15:6). The love commandment is grounded: 'As the Father has loved me, so have I loved you. Abide in my love' (15:9). 'Greater love has no one than this, that someone lay down his life for his friends (philoi)' (15:13). The disciples are elevated from 'servants' (douloi) to 'friends' (philoi) -- 'for the servant does not know what his master is doing; but I have called you friends, for all that I have heard from my Father I have made known to you' (15:15). The divine council's secrets are shared with the friends of God. Then the warning: the world will hate the disciples as it hated Jesus (15:18-25). 'If they persecuted me, they will also persecute you' (15:20). 'They hated me without a cause' (15:25, quoting Ps 69:4). The Parakletos is the witness who will testify about Jesus alongside the disciples' testimony (15:26-27).

sections.heading: The Parakletos's Ministry and Overcoming the World (16:1-33)
sections.body: Jesus warns of coming persecution: expulsion from synagogues and even death (16:2) -- 'the hour is coming when whoever kills you will think he is offering service (latreian) to God' (16:2). The departure is necessary: 'If I do not go away, the Parakletos will not come to you. But if I go, I will send him to you' (16:7). The Spirit's threefold ministry of conviction is described: 'He will convict the world concerning sin, because they do not believe in me; concerning righteousness, because I go to the Father and you will see me no longer; concerning judgment, because the ruler of this world is judged (kekritai)' (16:8-11). The Spirit proves: (a) the world's fundamental sin is unbelief; (b) Jesus' departure to the Father vindicates his righteousness; (c) the hostile archon's judgment is decided. The Spirit 'will guide you into all the truth (pasan ten aletheian)' and 'will declare to you the things that are to come' (16:13). 'He will glorify me, for he will take what is mine and declare it to you' (16:14). The Spirit's role is christocentric -- everything points to Jesus. The 'little while' (mikron) teaching (16:16-22) promises that sorrow will turn to joy, like a woman in labor. 'In that day you will ask in my name' (16:26) -- direct access to the Father through the Son. The discourse climaxes: 'I came from the Father and have come into the world, and now I am leaving the world and going to the Father' (16:28). The round trip: from the divine council to the world and back. The disciples affirm belief (16:30). Jesus warns of coming scattering (16:32) and closes with the declaration of cosmic victory: 'In the world you will have tribulation (thlipsis). But take heart (tharseite); I have overcome (nenikeka) the world' (16:33). The perfect tense nenikeka -- the victory is already won, even before the cross.

john_glory.title: The High Priestly Prayer -- Pre-Existent Glory and the Unity of God's People
john_glory.synopsis: John 17 is the longest recorded prayer of Jesus in the Gospels and one of the most theologically profound passages in the Bible. Traditionally called the 'High Priestly Prayer' (a designation from the 16th-century Lutheran theologian David Chytraeus), it functions as Jesus' intercession before his sacrificial death -- the prayer of the priest who is also the sacrifice. The prayer falls into three movements: (1) Jesus prays for himself -- for the restoration of his pre-incarnate glory (17:1-5); (2) Jesus prays for his disciples -- for their protection, unity, and sanctification (17:6-19); (3) Jesus prays for all future believers -- for their oneness, that the world may believe (17:20-26). The divine council implications are extraordinary. Jesus speaks of 'the glory (doxa) that I had with you (para soi) before the world existed (pro tou ton kosmon einai)' (17:5) -- personal glory shared with the Father before creation. He says the Father loved him 'before the foundation of the world (pro kataboles kosmou)' (17:24). He speaks of those the Father 'gave me out of the world' (17:6) and asks for their protection from 'the evil one' (tou ponerou -- 17:15). He prays: 'Sanctify them in the truth; your word (logos) is truth' (17:17). The unity prayer is cosmic: 'that they may all be one, just as you, Father, are in me, and I in you, that they also may be in us, so that the world may believe that you sent me' (17:21). The glory the Father gave the Son is shared with believers: 'The glory that you have given me I have given to them, that they may be one even as we are one' (17:22). The prayer ends with the desire that believers would see the Son's glory -- 'my glory that you have given me because you loved me before the foundation of the world' (17:24) -- and with the declaration: 'I made known to them your name (onoma), and I will continue to make it known' (17:26).

key_verse.text: 'And now, Father, glorify me in your own presence with the glory that I had with you before the world existed.'... 'Father, I desire that they also, whom you have given me, may be with me where I am, to see my glory that you have given me because you loved me before the foundation of the world.'

original_terms: doxa (glory -- the visible manifestation of the divine presence; the kavod of the OT; Jesus had this glory WITH the Father before creation and now asks for its restoration)
pro tou ton kosmon einai (before the world existed -- 17:5; a claim to pre-existence that transcends any prophetic or angelic category; the Son existed in glory before creation)
pro kataboles kosmou (before the foundation of the world -- 17:24; the Father's love for the Son predates all created reality; the relationship is eternal, not temporal)
onoma (name -- 17:6, 11, 12, 26; the divine name revealed to the disciples; Jesus has 'manifested' and 'made known' the Father's name -- the name 'I AM')
hagiason (sanctify/consecrate -- 17:17, 19; 'set apart for holy purpose'; Jesus consecrates himself as the sacrifice and asks the Father to consecrate the disciples for their mission)
ho poneros (the evil one -- 17:15; Jesus prays for protection from the hostile spiritual power; parallel to 'the ruler of this world')
hen (one -- 17:11, 21, 22, 23; the unity of believers modeled on the Father-Son unity; neuter hen, indicating unity of purpose/love/essence, not personal identity)
john_glory.ane_backdrop: The High Priestly Prayer reflects the ANE pattern of the king-priest who intercedes for his people before the gods. In Mesopotamia, the king functioned as mediator between the divine realm and humanity, offering prayers and sacrifices on behalf of the people. In Israel, the high priest entered the Holy of Holies once a year on the Day of Atonement (Lev 16) to make atonement for the people's sins. Jesus' prayer combines the royal and priestly roles: he is the king who intercedes (the Psalm 110:4 priest-king after the order of Melchizedek) and the sacrifice who is about to be offered (the Passover lamb of 1:29). The prayer's language of 'name' (onoma) recalls the ANE understanding of the divine name as the carrier of the deity's identity, authority, and presence. To 'manifest the name' (17:6) is to reveal the divine nature itself.

second_temple.summary: 'He is able to save to the uttermost those who draw near to God through him, since he always lives to make intercession for them.'
second_temple.relevance: The ongoing heavenly intercession of Christ is the fulfillment of the High Priestly Prayer. John 17 is not a one-time prayer but the earthly expression of the Son's eternal intercession at the Father's right hand.

second_temple.summary: 'The LORD possessed me at the beginning of his work, the first of his acts of old... before the beginning of the earth... then I was beside him, like a master workman.'
second_temple.relevance: Wisdom's pre-existence 'before the beginning of the earth' parallels Jesus' glory 'before the world existed' (17:5). The Logos prologue and the High Priestly Prayer together identify Jesus with the personified Wisdom of Proverbs who was with God at creation.

second_temple.summary: 'At that hour, that Son of Man was named in the presence of the Lord of Spirits, and his name before the Head of Days. Even before the sun and the signs were created, before the stars of heaven were made, his name was named before the Lord of Spirits.'
second_temple.relevance: The Enochic Son of Man is named before creation, paralleling Jesus' glory and the Father's love 'before the foundation of the world' (17:24). The pre-existence of the messianic figure was a live concept in Second Temple Judaism.

cross_refs.note: Wisdom before creation, 'beside him like a master workman' -- the background for Jesus' pre-existent glory (17:5)
Moses asks to see God's glory (kavod) -- Jesus possessed this glory before creation (17:5) and prays that believers will see it (17:24)
The Day of Atonement: the high priest enters the Holy of Holies to make atonement -- Jesus as both high priest and sacrifice
'You are a priest forever after the order of Melchizedek' -- the priest-king role that Jesus exercises in the High Priestly Prayer
'He always lives to make intercession for them' -- the ongoing heavenly ministry foreshadowed by John 17
'Christ Jesus is the one who died -- more than that, who was raised -- who is at the right hand of God, who indeed is interceding for us'
'He is before all things' -- the pre-existence affirmed in the High Priestly Prayer (17:5, 24)
'The one who was born of God protects him, and the evil one does not touch him... the whole world lies in the power of the evil one' -- the protection from 'the evil one' that Jesus prays for (17:15)

john_glory.divine_council_note: John 17 is the most sustained revelation of the Son's relationship to the Father and to the divine council in the entire New Testament. Three claims are of supreme importance. First, pre-existent glory: 'Glorify me in your own presence with the glory that I had WITH you (para soi) before the world existed' (17:5). The Son had personal, shared glory with the Father before creation. This is not the glory of a created being elevated to divine status but the glory of one who was 'with God and was God' (1:1) from eternity. The 'para soi' ('alongside you,' 'in your presence') echoes the 'pros ton theon' ('with God,' 'toward God') of 1:1 -- the same face-to-face relationship within the divine council. Second, the name: 'I have manifested your name (onoma) to the people whom you gave me' (17:6). 'Holy Father, keep them in your name, which you have given me' (17:11). The Father's name has been 'given' to the Son -- the Son bears the divine name. This echoes Exodus 23:20-21, where the Angel of YHWH bears YHWH's name ('my name is in him'). Jesus has 'manifested' (revealed, made visible) the Father's name -- the name 'I AM' (ehyeh/ego eimi) that he has been claiming throughout the Gospel. Third, the shared glory with believers: 'The glory that you have given me I have given to them, that they may be one even as we are one, I in them and you in me, that they may become perfectly one' (17:22-23). The divine council pattern is being extended: the glory of the Son is shared with believers so that they participate in the Father-Son unity. This is not deification in the pagan sense but the fulfillment of the divine image mandate of Genesis 1:26-28 -- humanity restored to its intended position 'a little lower than the elohim' (Ps 8:5), sharing in the divine glory through union with the Son.

sections.heading: Prayer for Himself -- The Restoration of Pre-Incarnate Glory (17:1-5)
sections.body: 'Father, the hour has come. Glorify your Son that the Son may glorify you' (17:1). The mutual glorification of Father and Son is the purpose of the cross: the Son's death glorifies the Father (by accomplishing salvation), and the Father glorifies the Son (by raising him and restoring him to heavenly glory). 'You have given him authority over all flesh, to give eternal life to all whom you have given him' (17:2). The authority echoes Daniel 7:14 -- the Son of Man receiving universal dominion. 'This is eternal life, that they know you, the only true God, and Jesus Christ whom you have sent' (17:3). Eternal life IS knowledge of the Father and the Son -- relational, experiential, intimate knowledge. 'I glorified you on earth, having accomplished (teleiosas) the work that you gave me to do' (17:4). The perfect tense anticipates the cross's 'tetelestai' (19:30) -- the work is as good as done. 'And now, Father, glorify me in your own presence (para seauto) with the glory that I had with you (para soi) before the world existed (pro tou ton kosmon einai)' (17:5). This verse alone demolishes any christology that treats Jesus as merely a prophet, a teacher, or a created being. The Son existed in glory WITH the Father BEFORE the world was made. He is asking for the restoration of what he voluntarily laid aside in the incarnation (cf. Phil 2:6-7).

sections.heading: Prayer for the Disciples -- Protection, Unity, Sanctification (17:6-19)
sections.body: 'I have manifested your name to the people whom you gave me out of the world. Yours they were, and you gave them to me, and they have kept your word' (17:6). The disciples are described as the Father's gift to the Son -- chosen before they chose. 'I am not praying for the world but for those whom you have given me, for they are yours' (17:9). The prayer is specifically for the disciples who will carry the mission forward. 'Holy Father, keep them in your name, which you have given me, that they may be one, even as we are one' (17:11). The disciples are kept 'in the name' -- protected by the divine name that the Son bears. Unity among the disciples is modeled on the unity of Father and Son. 'I do not ask that you take them out of the world, but that you keep them from the evil one (tou ponerou)' (17:15). The prayer is not for escape but for protection in the midst of hostile territory -- the world under the ruler's domain. 'Sanctify them in the truth; your word (logos) is truth' (17:17). Truth sanctifies. 'As you sent me into the world, so I have sent them into the world' (17:18). The sending pattern: Father -> Son -> disciples. 'For their sake I consecrate (hagiazo) myself, that they also may be sanctified in truth' (17:19). Jesus consecrates himself as the sacrifice -- the priestly self-offering that makes the disciples' mission possible.

sections.heading: Prayer for All Believers -- Oneness and Glory (17:20-26)
sections.body: 'I do not ask for these only, but also for those who will believe in me through their word' (17:20). The prayer extends to all future believers across all centuries. 'That they may all be one, just as you, Father, are in me, and I in you, that they also may be in us, so that the world may believe that you sent me' (17:21). The unity of believers is the apologetic evidence for the gospel -- the world will believe when it sees a community that reflects the Father-Son unity. 'The glory that you have given me I have given to them, that they may be one even as we are one, I in them and you in me, that they may become perfectly one (teteleiomenoi eis hen), so that the world may know that you sent me and loved them even as you loved me' (17:22-23). Believers share in the divine glory -- the glory of the divine council's throne room is extended to the community of faith. 'Father, I desire that they also, whom you have given me, may be with me where I am, to see my glory that you have given me because you loved me before the foundation of the world (pro kataboles kosmou)' (17:24). The ultimate desire: that believers would be in the Son's presence and see his pre-existent glory -- the glory of the eternal council. 'I made known to them your name (onoma), and I will continue to make it known, that the love with which you have loved me may be in them, and I in them' (17:26). The prayer ends as it began: with the divine name, the divine love, and the divine indwelling.

john_glory.title: The Trial and Crucifixion -- It Is Finished (Tetelestai)
john_glory.synopsis: Chapters 18-19 narrate the arrest, trial, and crucifixion of Jesus -- but in John's theology, these are not events of defeat but of triumph. Jesus goes to the cross with sovereign authority: 'I lay down my life of my own accord' (10:18). The arrest scene (18:1-11) includes a remarkable detail unique to John: when the soldiers say they seek 'Jesus of Nazareth,' he responds 'ego eimi' (I AM -- 18:5), and 'they drew back and fell to the ground' (18:6). Even at his arrest, the divine name carries power. The trial before Pilate (18:28-19:16) is the most extensive in any Gospel. The theological center is Jesus' declaration: 'My kingdom is not of this world (he basileia he eme ouk estin ek tou kosmou toutou)' (18:36). Pilate asks: 'So you are a king?' Jesus answers: 'You say that I am a king. For this purpose I was born and for this purpose I have come into the world -- to bear witness to the truth' (18:37). Pilate presents Jesus to the crowd with the words 'Ecce Homo' -- 'Behold the Man!' (Ide ho anthropos -- 19:5). The crucifixion (19:17-37) is narrated with theological precision. The inscription reads 'Jesus of Nazareth, the King of the Jews' in Hebrew, Latin, and Greek (19:19-20). Pilate refuses to change it: 'What I have written I have written' (19:22). Jesus' seamless tunic is not torn but cast by lots (19:23-24), fulfilling Psalm 22:18. He commits his mother to the Beloved Disciple (19:26-27). He says 'I thirst' (19:28) and is given sour wine on a hyssop branch (19:29) -- hyssop was used to apply the Passover lamb's blood (Exod 12:22). Then the final word: 'Tetelestai' -- 'It is finished' (19:30). Not a cry of despair but a shout of completion. The soldiers do not break his legs (fulfilling Exod 12:46 / Ps 34:20) but pierce his side, and 'at once there came out blood and water' (19:34). John emphasizes the eyewitness testimony: 'He who saw it has borne witness -- his testimony is true' (19:35). Joseph of Arimathea and Nicodemus (who first came 'by night,' 3:2) bury Jesus with 75 pounds of myrrh and aloes (19:39-40) -- a royal burial.

key_verse.text: When Jesus had received the sour wine, he said, 'It is finished,' and he bowed his head and gave up his spirit.

original_terms: ego eimi (I AM -- 18:5-6, 8; Jesus' response to the arrest party; the soldiers fall to the ground at the utterance of the divine name; even at his arrest, he is sovereign)
he basileia he eme (my kingdom -- 18:36; not 'of this world'; not originating from the world-system under the ruler's authority but from the divine council's realm)
ho anthropos (the man -- 19:5; 'Behold the Man!' Pilate's ironic declaration; the divine Logos presented in his full humanity, the 'son of man' on display)

tetelestai (it is finished/accomplished -- 19:30; perfect passive tense: 'it has been and remains completed'; the divine mission is accomplished; the commercial usage means 'paid in full'; the sacrificial work of the Lamb is done)
hyssopos (hyssop -- 19:29; the branch used to apply the Passover lamb's blood to the doorposts in Exodus 12:22; John connects the crucifixion to the Passover sacrifice)
ostoun ou syntribesetai (a bone of him shall not be broken -- 19:36; quoting Exod 12:46 and/or Ps 34:20; Jesus as the unblemished Passover lamb)
john_glory.ane_backdrop: Roman crucifixion was designed as the ultimate public humiliation -- the victim was stripped naked, displayed at major thoroughfares, and left to die of asphyxiation over hours or days. The crucified person was considered cursed: Deuteronomy 21:23 states 'a hanged man is cursed by God' (Paul develops this in Gal 3:13). The trilingual inscription (Hebrew, Latin, Greek -- 19:20) may correspond to the three major civilizations of the ancient world: Jewish, Roman, and Greek -- the crucified king is proclaimed to all cultures. The piercing of Jesus' side (19:34) and the flow of 'blood and water' has been interpreted medically (pericardial fluid), sacramentally (eucharist and baptism), and typologically (the water and blood that flowed from the rock in the wilderness, Exod 17; Num 20). The 75 pounds of burial spices (19:39) is a royal quantity -- Josephus records that 500 servants carried spices at Herod's funeral.

second_temple.summary: 'You shall not break any of its bones' -- the Passover lamb regulation.
second_temple.relevance: John explicitly quotes this (19:36) as fulfilled in the soldiers' decision not to break Jesus' legs. Jesus dies as the Passover lamb -- at the very hour the lambs were being slaughtered in the Temple (John's chronology places the crucifixion on the 14th of Nisan, the day of preparation).

second_temple.summary: 'They will look on me, on him whom they have pierced, and they shall mourn for him, as one mourns for an only child.'
second_temple.relevance: John quotes this (19:37) in connection with the piercing of Jesus' side. The remarkable feature of Zechariah's prophecy is the first-person divine speaker: 'they will look on ME... whom THEY have pierced.' YHWH speaks of being pierced -- another 'two powers' text: the divine figure who is pierced is both YHWH and a distinct figure mourned 'as an only child.'

second_temple.summary: 'They divide my garments among them, and for my clothing they cast lots.'
second_temple.relevance: Fulfilled in the soldiers' division of Jesus' garments and the casting of lots for his seamless tunic (19:23-24). Psalm 22 is the great crucifixion psalm, though John does not quote the opening cry ('My God, my God, why have you forsaken me?' -- reserved for the Synoptics).

second_temple.summary: 'He was pierced for our transgressions... like a lamb that is led to the slaughter, and like a sheep that before its shearers is silent, so he opened not his mouth.'
second_temple.relevance: The Suffering Servant pierced for transgressions, silent before accusers -- the prophetic template fulfilled in John's Passion narrative.

cross_refs.note: The Passover lamb: hyssop for applying blood, no bone broken -- Jesus dies as the Passover lamb at the hour of sacrifice
'They will look on me, whom they have pierced' -- the YHWH who is pierced; quoted in 19:37
'They divide my garments... for my clothing they cast lots' -- fulfilled in 19:23-24
'He keeps all his bones; not one of them is broken' -- fulfilled in the decision not to break Jesus' legs (19:36)
The Suffering Servant 'pierced for our transgressions,' silent before accusers -- the prophetic template for the Passion
'Christ redeemed us from the curse of the law by becoming a curse for us' -- theological interpretation of the crucifixion
'Every eye will see him, even those who pierced him, and all tribes of the earth will wail on account of him' -- Zech 12:10 applied to the second coming
'Christ, our Passover lamb, has been sacrificed' -- Paul's explicit identification, which John's chronology and symbolism support

john_glory.divine_council_note: The Passion narrative in John is presented as the divine council's sovereign action, not human power overwhelming Jesus. Three details demonstrate this. First, the ego eimi at the arrest (18:5-6): when Jesus says 'I AM,' the soldiers 'drew back and fell to the ground.' Even at the moment of his arrest, the divine name carries the power to prostrate his enemies. Jesus is not captured; he surrenders -- 'I lay down my life of my own accord' (10:18). Second, 'My kingdom is not of this world' (18:36): the Son's kingdom does not originate from the world-system under the archon's rule. It originates from the divine council's realm -- 'from above' (anothen, cf. 3:3, 31; 8:23). Third, tetelestai (19:30): the final word is not a cry of defeat but a declaration of mission accomplished. The verb teleo means 'to complete, to accomplish, to bring to its goal (telos).' The divine council's plan -- the judgment of the ruler of this world (12:31), the casting out of the hostile archon (12:31), the drawing of all people to the lifted-up Son (12:32) -- is accomplished. The perfect tense (tetelestai, 'it has been and remains finished') indicates permanent, irreversible completion. Additionally, John's Passover chronology (Jesus dies on the 14th of Nisan, when the lambs are slaughtered) makes explicit what the Baptist proclaimed: 'Behold, the Lamb of God, who takes away the sin of the world' (1:29). The divine council's plan from before the world's foundation (17:24) reaches its climax at the hour determined before time began.

sections.heading: The Arrest and the Divine Name (18:1-11)
sections.body: Jesus crosses the Kidron Valley (a detail evoking David's flight from Absalom, 2 Sam 15:23) to a garden. Judas arrives with a 'band of soldiers and some officers from the chief priests and the Pharisees' (18:3) -- the speira (cohort) may suggest Roman military involvement from the start. Jesus, 'knowing all that would happen to him' (18:4), steps forward: 'Whom do you seek?' 'Jesus of Nazareth.' 'Ego eimi' (18:5). 'When Jesus said to them, "ego eimi," they drew back and fell to the ground' (18:6). This is a theophany at the point of arrest: the divine name spoken by the incarnate Logos has physical force, prostrating the arrest party. Jesus asks again, repeats ego eimi, and says: 'If you seek me, let these men go' (18:8) -- protecting the disciples, fulfilling his own prayer (17:12). Peter strikes with a sword, cutting off the ear of Malchus (18:10); Jesus stops him: 'Shall I not drink the cup that the Father has given me?' (18:11) -- the cup of judgment, voluntarily received.

sections.heading: The Trial Before Pilate (18:28-19:16)
sections.body: The trial moves between outside (where Pilate addresses the Jews) and inside (where Pilate interrogates Jesus) in a dramatic literary structure. Pilate asks: 'Are you the King of the Jews?' Jesus responds: 'Do you say this of your own accord, or did others say it to you about me?' (18:34). Pilate: 'Am I a Jew?' Jesus: 'My kingdom is not of this world. If my kingdom were of this world, my servants would have been fighting, that I might not be delivered over to the Jews. But my kingdom is not from here (enteuthen -- from here)' (18:36). The kingdom originates from a different realm -- the divine council's domain. Pilate: 'So you are a king?' Jesus: 'You say that I am a king. For this purpose I was born and for this purpose I have come into the world (eis ton kosmon) -- to bear witness to the truth' (18:37). 'I have come into the world' echoes the descent language of the entire Gospel (3:13; 6:38; 16:28). Pilate's 'What is truth?' (18:38) is the tragic irony: truth incarnate stands before him, and he does not recognize it. The Barabbas exchange (18:39-40), the scourging (19:1), the crown of thorns and purple robe (19:2-3) -- each detail is ironic: the mock kingship is the real kingship. Pilate presents Jesus: 'Ide ho anthropos' -- 'Behold the Man!' (19:5). Then: 'Ide ho basileus hymon' -- 'Behold your King!' (19:14). The chief priests: 'We have no king but Caesar' (19:15) -- the ultimate apostasy, renouncing YHWH's kingship. Pilate delivers Jesus to be crucified.

sections.heading: The Crucifixion -- It Is Finished (19:17-42)
sections.body: Jesus carries his own cross (no Simon of Cyrene in John) to Golgotha. The inscription in three languages -- 'Jesus of Nazareth, the King of the Jews' (19:19) -- proclaims his kingship to the whole known world. The seamless tunic (19:23) -- 'woven in one piece from top to bottom' -- may symbolize the high priest's garment (Josephus, Antiquities 3.161), further identifying Jesus as the priest-king. The soldiers cast lots for it, fulfilling Psalm 22:18 (19:24). Jesus commits his mother to the Beloved Disciple (19:26-27) -- the creation of a new family. 'I thirst' (19:28) fulfills Psalm 69:21. He is given sour wine on a hyssop branch (19:29) -- hyssop connects to the Passover: it was used to apply the lamb's blood to the doorposts (Exod 12:22). Then: 'Tetelestai' -- 'It is finished' (19:30). This is John's replacement for the Synoptics' cry of dereliction ('My God, my God, why have you forsaken me?'). In John's theology, the cross is not abandonment but completion -- the telos toward which the entire Gospel has moved. 'He bowed his head and gave up his spirit (paredoken to pneuma)' (19:30) -- the voluntary handing over of his spirit. The soldiers break the legs of the two others but not Jesus' (he is already dead), fulfilling the Passover lamb regulation (19:36; Exod 12:46). A soldier pierces his side: 'at once there came out blood and water' (19:34). John appeals to eyewitness testimony (19:35). He cites Zechariah 12:10: 'They will look on him whom they have pierced' (19:37) -- the remarkable prophecy where YHWH speaks of being pierced. Joseph of Arimathea and Nicodemus (now acting openly, no longer 'by night') provide a royal burial with 75 pounds of spices in a new tomb in a garden (19:38-42).

john_glory.title: The Resurrection -- My Lord and My God
john_glory.synopsis: Chapters 20-21 narrate the resurrection of Jesus and its aftermath -- the climax of the Gospel's theological argument and the vindication of every claim Jesus has made. Chapter 20 opens with Mary Magdalene coming to the tomb 'while it was still dark (skotias)' (20:1) -- the darkness that will give way to the light. She finds the stone removed and runs to tell Peter and the Beloved Disciple. They race to the tomb: Peter enters and sees the linen cloths lying there, and the face cloth 'folded up in a place by itself' (20:7). The Beloved Disciple enters, 'saw and believed' (20:8). Mary remains at the tomb weeping and sees two angels (20:12). Jesus appears to her but she mistakes him for the gardener (20:15) -- until he speaks her name: 'Mary' (Mariam -- 20:16). She responds: 'Rabboni!' (Teacher). Jesus says: 'Do not cling to me, for I have not yet ascended to the Father; but go to my brothers and say to them, "I am ascending to my Father and your Father, to my God and your God"' (20:17). That evening, Jesus appears to the disciples behind locked doors, shows his hands and side, and says: 'Peace be with you. As the Father has sent me, even so I am sending you' (20:21). He breathes on them and says: 'Receive the Holy Spirit (labete pneuma hagion)' (20:22). Thomas is absent and declares he will not believe unless he can touch the wounds (20:25). Eight days later, Jesus appears again. Thomas sees and confesses: 'Ho kyrios mou kai ho theos mou' -- 'My Lord and my God!' (20:28). Jesus responds: 'Have you believed because you have seen me? Blessed are those who have not seen and yet have believed' (20:29). John states the Gospel's purpose: 'These are written so that you may believe that Jesus is the Christ, the Son of God, and that by believing you may have life in his name' (20:31). Chapter 21 (possibly added by a later editor, 21:24-25) narrates the appearance at the Sea of Tiberias: the miraculous catch of 153 fish (21:11), breakfast on the shore, and the threefold restoration of Peter (21:15-17) -- 'Do you love me? Feed my sheep' -- reversing his threefold denial. Jesus predicts Peter's death (21:18-19) and addresses the Beloved Disciple's future (21:20-23). The Gospel closes: 'There are also many other things that Jesus did. Were every one of them to be written, I suppose that the world itself could not contain the books that would be written' (21:25).

key_verse.text: Thomas answered him, 'My Lord and my God!' Jesus said to him, 'Have you believed because you have seen me? Blessed are those who have not seen and yet have believed.'... Now Jesus did many other signs in the presence of the disciples, which are not written in this book; but these are written so that you may believe that Jesus is the Christ, the Son of God, and that by believing you may have life in his name.

original_terms: ho kyrios mou kai ho theos mou (my Lord and my God -- 20:28; the christological climax of the Gospel; kyrios = YHWH in the LXX; theos = elohim; Thomas confesses Jesus as both YHWH and Elohim; the 'two powers in heaven' confessed as one)
Rabboni (my Teacher/my Master -- 20:16; Aramaic, a more intimate form of Rabbi; Mary's initial recognition before Jesus reveals the fuller reality of his resurrection)
pneuma hagion (Holy Spirit -- 20:22; Jesus breathes the Spirit on the disciples, echoing God breathing life into Adam in Gen 2:7; a new creation act)
emphysao (breathed on -- 20:22; used in the LXX only in Gen 2:7, where God breathes life into Adam; Jesus as Creator performs a new creation by breathing the Spirit)

agapas me (do you love me -- 21:15-17; the threefold question reversing Peter's threefold denial; the shift between agapao and phileo has been debated but may not carry theological distinction in John's usage)
boske ta probata mou (feed my sheep -- 21:16-17; the pastoral commission; Peter is restored as under-shepherd of the flock whose Good Shepherd is Jesus)
john_glory.ane_backdrop: The empty tomb narrative engages the ancient world's understanding of death and afterlife. In Greco-Roman culture, the dead descended to Hades, a shadowy realm from which return was nearly impossible (Odysseus' journey to the underworld, Orpheus and Eurydice). In Egyptian religion, the dead faced judgment and could attain blessed afterlife through proper rituals and the favor of Osiris. In Jewish tradition, resurrection was a late development: Daniel 12:2 is the clearest OT statement, and the Pharisees affirmed bodily resurrection while the Sadducees denied it (Acts 23:8). Jesus' resurrection in John is emphatically physical -- he shows his wounds (20:20, 27), eats breakfast (21:12-13), and can be touched (20:27) -- yet his body has transcended normal physical limitations (he appears in locked rooms, 20:19, 26). The breathing of the Spirit (20:22) echoes Genesis 2:7, where God breathed (emphysao in the LXX) the breath of life into Adam. Jesus' act is a new creation: the risen Lord bestows the Spirit of the new creation on his disciples.

second_temple.summary: 'And God formed man of dust from the ground, and breathed (enephysesen) into his nostrils the breath of life, and man became a living being.'
second_temple.relevance: Jesus' act of breathing the Spirit on the disciples (20:22) uses the same Greek verb (emphysao). The risen Christ performs a new creation: as God breathed physical life into Adam, Jesus breathes spiritual life into the new humanity.

second_temple.summary: 'The LORD said to my Lord: "Sit at my right hand, until I make your enemies your footstool."'
second_temple.relevance: Thomas's confession 'my Lord (ho kyrios mou)' echoes the Psalm 110:1 'my Lord' (adoni in Hebrew, ho kyrios mou in the LXX). Thomas recognizes Jesus as the figure at YHWH's right hand -- David's Lord, the second YHWH figure.

second_temple.summary: 'Christ died for our sins in accordance with the Scriptures, he was buried, he was raised on the third day in accordance with the Scriptures, and he appeared to Cephas, then to the twelve.'
second_temple.relevance: Paul's early creed confirms the resurrection tradition. John's account provides the narrative detail: the empty tomb, the grave clothes, the appearances to Mary, the disciples, and Thomas.

second_temple.summary: 'Let all the house of Israel therefore know for certain that God has made him both Lord (kyrios) and Christ (christos), this Jesus whom you crucified.'
second_temple.relevance: Thomas's confession 'my Lord and my God' (20:28) is the personal, experiential version of Peter's proclamation. The resurrection vindicates Jesus as kyrios (= YHWH) and christos (= Messiah).

cross_refs.note: God breathed life into Adam -- Jesus breathes the Spirit on the disciples (20:22), performing a new creation
'The LORD said to my Lord' -- Thomas's 'my Lord' (20:28) echoes the Psalm's 'my Lord' at YHWH's right hand
'I saw the Lord high and lifted up' -- Isaiah saw the pre-incarnate Logos (cf. 12:41); Thomas now sees him risen
'Prophesy to the breath... Come from the four winds, O breath, and breathe on these slain, that they may live' -- the Spirit's life-giving power, fulfilled in 20:22
Paul's early resurrection creed: Christ died, was buried, was raised, and appeared -- the tradition John narrates in detail
'God has made him both Lord and Christ' -- the resurrection vindicates Jesus as kyrios and christos; Thomas confesses this personally (20:28)
'If you confess with your mouth that Jesus is Lord' -- Thomas's confession is the paradigm of saving faith
'I am the first and the last, and the living one. I died, and behold I am alive forevermore' -- the risen Christ's self-identification
'Shepherd the flock of God... and when the chief Shepherd appears, you will receive the unfading crown of glory' -- the pastoral commission of 21:15-17 extended

john_glory.divine_council_note: The resurrection and Thomas's confession form the christological inclusio of the entire Gospel. The Gospel opened with 'the Word was God' (theos en ho logos, 1:1); it concludes with Thomas confessing 'my God' (ho theos mou, 20:28). The theological journey is complete: the claim made in the prologue has been vindicated by the resurrection and confessed by a human witness. Thomas's confession uses both divine titles of the LXX: kyrios (the Greek translation of YHWH, the personal covenant name of God) and theos (the Greek translation of elohim, the generic divine title). In the divine council framework, YHWH and Elohim are the two titles that distinguish the God of Israel from the other elohim of the council. Thomas applies BOTH to Jesus, confessing him as the God of Israel -- the YHWH who presides over the council and the Elohim who is above all elohim. Jesus does not correct Thomas; he affirms the confession and extends it: 'Blessed are those who have not seen and yet have believed' (20:29). The breathing of the Spirit (20:22) is a divine council act: as YHWH breathed life into Adam at creation (Gen 2:7), the risen Christ breathes the Spirit of new creation into the disciples. The one 'through whom all things were made' (1:3) is now creating anew. The commission 'As the Father has sent me, even so I am sending you' (20:21) establishes the chain of sending from the divine council: the Father sends the Son from the heavenly throne room into the world; the Son sends the disciples from the upper room into the world; the Spirit empowers the mission. The restoration of Peter (21:15-17) installs him as under-shepherd of the flock whose Good Shepherd (10:11) has been raised from the dead. The Gospel's final word is that the things Jesus did are inexhaustible: 'the world itself could not contain the books that would be written' (21:25). The Logos who created the world cannot be fully contained by the world he made.

sections.heading: The Empty Tomb and the Risen Lord (20:1-18)
sections.body: Mary Magdalene comes to the tomb 'while it was still dark (skotias eti ouses)' (20:1). In John's symbolic world, darkness represents unbelief and the domain of the ruler of this world -- Mary comes in darkness and will leave in light. She finds the stone removed and runs to Peter and the Beloved Disciple: 'They have taken the Lord out of the tomb' (20:2). The two disciples race to the tomb; the Beloved Disciple arrives first but waits; Peter enters and sees the linen cloths lying there, and the face cloth 'not lying with the linen cloths but folded up in a place by itself' (20:7). The orderly arrangement argues against theft -- grave robbers do not fold burial cloths. The Beloved Disciple enters, 'saw and believed' (20:8) -- the disciple closest to Jesus is the first to believe in the resurrection. Mary, still weeping at the tomb, sees two angels in white (20:12). She turns and sees Jesus but does not recognize him -- until he speaks her name: 'Mary (Mariam)' (20:16). The Good Shepherd 'calls his own sheep by name' (10:3) -- Mary recognizes the shepherd's voice (10:4, 27). She responds: 'Rabboni!' and presumably reaches for him, because Jesus says: 'Do not cling to me (me mou haptou), for I have not yet ascended to the Father' (20:17). The ascension to the Father is still to come -- the full return to the pre-incarnate glory (17:5). Jesus tells her: 'I am ascending to my Father and your Father, to my God and your God' (20:17). Note: 'MY Father and YOUR Father' -- not 'our Father.' The Son's relationship to the Father is unique; believers' relationship is derived from his. Mary becomes 'the apostle to the apostles,' announcing the resurrection to the disciples (20:18).

sections.heading: Thomas's Confession -- My Lord and My God (20:19-31)
sections.body: That evening, Jesus appears to the disciples behind locked doors: 'Peace be with you (eirene hymin)' (20:19). He shows them his hands and side. 'The disciples were glad when they saw the Lord' (20:20). Then the commission: 'As the Father has sent me, even so I am sending you (kagō pempō hymas)' (20:21). The Father's sending of the Son is the model for the Son's sending of the disciples -- from the divine council's realm into the world. 'He breathed on them (enephysesen) and said to them, "Receive the Holy Spirit"' (20:22). The verb enephysesen appears in the LXX only in Genesis 2:7 -- the Creator breathing life into the first human. The risen Christ performs a new Genesis: the Spirit of new creation is breathed into the new humanity. Thomas, absent from this appearance, refuses to believe without physical evidence: 'Unless I see in his hands the mark of the nails, and place my finger into the mark of the nails, and place my hand into his side, I will never believe' (20:25). Eight days later, Jesus appears again and invites Thomas to touch: 'Put your finger here, and see my hands; and put out your hand, and place it in my side. Do not disbelieve (me ginou apistos), but believe (pistos)' (20:27). Thomas responds with the greatest christological confession in the New Testament: 'Ho kyrios mou kai ho theos mou' -- 'My Lord and my God!' (20:28). This is not an exclamation of surprise but a confession of faith addressed directly to Jesus (eipen auto -- 'he said TO HIM'). In the LXX, kyrios translates YHWH, and theos translates Elohim. Thomas confesses Jesus as both the YHWH who presides over the divine council and the Elohim who is above all elohim. Jesus affirms the confession and extends it: 'Blessed are those who have not seen and yet have believed' (20:29). The Gospel's purpose statement follows: 'These are written so that you may believe that Jesus is the Christ, the Son of God, and that by believing you may have life in his name' (20:31).

sections.heading: The Restoration of Peter and the Gospel's Close (21:1-25)
sections.body: Chapter 21 is widely regarded as an epilogue, possibly added by the final editor of the Gospel (the 'we' of 21:24). Jesus appears at the Sea of Tiberias to seven disciples who have been fishing all night without success (21:3). He calls from shore: 'Cast the net on the right side of the boat' (21:6). They haul in 153 large fish (21:11) -- a number whose significance has been debated for centuries: Jerome said it was the number of known fish species (the mission catches all nations); others see numerical symbolism or simply precise eyewitness memory. Jesus has prepared breakfast: 'Come and have breakfast (aristesate)' (21:12). The risen Lord serves his disciples -- the foot-washing servant continues to serve even in resurrection. The threefold restoration of Peter (21:15-17) mirrors his threefold denial (18:17, 25, 27). Three times Jesus asks: 'Do you love me?' Three times Peter affirms. Three times Jesus commissions: 'Feed my lambs... Tend my sheep... Feed my sheep.' The restoration is complete: the one who denied three times is three times commissioned as under-shepherd. Jesus predicts Peter's death: 'when you are old, you will stretch out your hands, and another will dress you and carry you where you do not wish to go' (21:18) -- traditionally interpreted as crucifixion. 'Follow me' (21:19). Peter asks about the Beloved Disciple; Jesus responds: 'If it is my will that he remain until I come, what is that to you? You follow me!' (21:22). The Gospel's final editorial note: 'This is the disciple who is bearing witness about these things, and who has written these things, and we know that his testimony is true' (21:24). The 'we' suggests a community affirming the Beloved Disciple's eyewitness authority. The Gospel closes with a hyperbolic flourish: 'Were every one of them to be written, I suppose that the world itself could not contain the books that would be written' (21:25). The Logos who made the world is too vast for the world to contain.

john_discourse.title: I AM -- The Divine Name in John
john_discourse.synopsis: The seven ego eimi statements with predicates form the christological backbone of John's Gospel. Each claim reaches into the Old Testament and pulls forward a divine attribute that Jesus applies to himself: 'I am the bread of life' (6:35) -- the manna from heaven that sustained Israel in the wilderness; 'I am the light of the world' (8:12) -- the pillar of fire that led Israel through darkness; 'I am the door of the sheep' (10:7) -- the only legitimate entry to the covenant flock; 'I am the good shepherd' (10:11) -- YHWH himself who shepherds Israel (Ezek 34:11-16); 'I am the resurrection and the life' (11:25) -- the one who holds the keys of death and Sheol; 'I am the way, the truth, and the life' (14:6) -- the exclusive mediator between God and humanity; 'I am the true vine' (15:1) -- replacing unfaithful Israel (Isa 5:1-7) as the source of covenant life. [A] Direct Scripture. But behind all seven stands the absolute ego eimi of John 8:58: 'Before Abraham was, I AM (ego eimi).' [A] This is not a predicated metaphor -- it is the divine name claim. The Jews picked up stones because they understood exactly what Jesus was saying: he was claiming to be the I AM of Exodus 3:14, the self-existent one who spoke to Moses from the burning bush. [B] The high priest's reaction at the trial (Mark 14:62-63) -- tearing his robes -- was not because Jesus claimed to be the Messiah (many did), but because he claimed to be the cloud-rider of Daniel 7:13, a prerogative belonging exclusively to YHWH in the Old Testament (Ps 68:4, 104:3, Deut 33:26, Isa 19:1).

key_verse.text: Jesus said to them, 'Truly, truly, I say to you, before Abraham was, I am.'

original_terms: ego eimi (I AM -- Greek rendering of the divine name; when Jesus uses it without a predicate in 8:58, it echoes the Hebrew ehyeh asher ehyeh, 'I AM WHO I AM,' of Exodus 3:14; the Septuagint renders Exodus 3:14 as ego eimi ho on, 'I am the one who is')

artos tes zoes (bread of life -- 6:35; artos = bread/loaf, zoe = life; Jesus as the true manna that gives eternal life, surpassing the wilderness manna that sustained the body temporarily; cf. Exod 16:4, Ps 78:24-25)

phos tou kosmou (light of the world -- 8:12; phos = light, kosmos = world/ordered creation; Jesus as the cosmic light that dispels the darkness of the fallen world; echoes the pillar of fire, Exod 13:21, and the Servant light, Isa 42:6, 49:6)

ho poimen ho kalos (the good shepherd -- 10:11; poimen = shepherd, kalos = good/ noble/beautiful; Jesus claims the role that Ezekiel 34 reserves for YHWH himself: 'I myself will search for my sheep... I myself will be the shepherd of my flock')

he anastasis kai he zoe (the resurrection and the life -- 11:25; anastasis = resurrection/rising up, zoe = life; Jesus does not merely promise resurrection -- he IS the resurrection; the power of life resides in his person)

he hodos kai he aletheia kai he zoe (the way, the truth, and the life -- 14:6; hodos = way/road/path, aletheia = truth/reality, zoe = life; three attributes in one declaration -- Jesus is the exclusive path to the Father)

he ampelos he alethine (the true vine -- 15:1; ampelos = vine/grapevine, alethinos = true/genuine/real; Israel was the vine that failed (Isa 5, Jer 2:21, Ezek 15); Jesus is the true vine that produces the fruit Israel never could)
john_discourse.ane_backdrop: The formula 'I am [deity]' was widespread in the ancient Near East as a self-revelation formula for gods. Egyptian texts record the sun god Re declaring 'I am Khepri in the morning, Re at noon, Atum in the evening' -- a divine self-identification pattern. In Mesopotamian hymns, Ishtar declares 'I am the queen of heaven and earth.' The Baal Cycle has Baal announce his identity through deeds of power. But the Hebrew pattern is unique: YHWH's 'I AM WHO I AM' (ehyeh asher ehyeh, Exod 3:14) is not a predicated identity ('I am the god of X') but an absolute self-existence claim -- the name derives from the verb 'to be' (hayah). When Jesus uses ego eimi absolutely (8:58, 18:6), he claims not merely divine status but the specific identity of YHWH. The shepherd imagery also has deep ANE roots: Mesopotamian kings were called 'shepherd of the people,' and the Code of Hammurabi opens with Hammurabi as the shepherd appointed by the gods. But Ezekiel 34 strips the title from human kings and reserves it for YHWH alone -- and Jesus claims that role directly.

second_temple.summary: This fragment describes a coming figure through whom God will 'heal the wounded, revive the dead, and bring good news to the poor.' It attributes resurrection power to a messianic agent.
second_temple.relevance: Jesus' claim 'I am the resurrection and the life' (11:25) fits within the Second Temple expectation of a messianic figure with divine power over death. But Jesus goes beyond the expectation: he does not merely channel God's resurrection power -- he IS the resurrection.

second_temple.source: Philo of Alexandria, On Dreams (De Somniis 1.229-230)
second_temple.summary: Philo interprets the burning bush theophany and the 'I AM' as God's declaration of absolute self-existence: God alone truly 'is,' and all created beings derive their existence from him.
second_temple.relevance: Philo's philosophical reflection on 'I AM' as a claim to absolute being illuminates how a first-century Jewish audience would hear Jesus' ego eimi in 8:58 -- as a direct claim to the divine attribute of self-existence.

second_temple.summary: YHWH declares through Isaiah: 'You are my witnesses... that you may know and believe me and understand that I am he (ego eimi). Before me no god was formed, nor shall there be any after me. I, I am the LORD (ego eimi), and besides me there is no savior.'
second_temple.relevance: The LXX uses the same ego eimi formula that Jesus uses in John 8:58. Isaiah's context is explicitly monotheistic: ego eimi is YHWH's exclusive self-designation. Jesus' use of the same formula in the same absolute way is a claim to be the speaker of Isaiah 43.

cross_refs.note: 'God said to Moses, I AM WHO I AM (ehyeh asher ehyeh)' -- the divine name that Jesus invokes with the absolute ego eimi of John 8:58
YHWH's ego eimi declarations in the LXX -- 'I am he' (ego eimi) -- the exclusive self-designation of the God of Israel that Jesus appropriates
'I myself will search for my sheep... I myself will be the shepherd of my flock' -- YHWH reclaims the shepherd role that Jesus claims in John 10:11
